query: OT/ICS solution engineer at an industrial cybersecurity vendor

positive_expectations.reason: Solution Engineer at Claroty (a leading OT/ICS security vendor), category=sales_engineering, 9 work history rows, 419 chars of skills, fresh embedding. If recall is functioning at all, he must surface in top 5 against ~175 plausible competitors (20 solution engineers + 156 sales engineers nationwide). No geographic filter is in play — he wins on profile signal alone or recall is broken.

negative_expectations.criteria: Pure IT security candidates (SOC analysts, IT cybersecurity engineers) with no OT/ICS/industrial signal in their profile
negative_expectations.reason: These are the most likely false positives because 'cybersecurity' alone is a strong keyword signal. A candidate with no OT exposure should not outrank an actual OT vendor SE.

negative_expectations.criteria: Sales engineers from non-security industries (e.g., generic SaaS, networking hardware, telecom)
negative_expectations.reason: Job title alone (sales engineer / solution engineer) without security domain context should not rank above John, whose entire profile is OT security.

reasoning: R1 baseline recall test per audit plan §5A and §5E. Tests whether the obvious match surfaces under realistic competition. Scope is candidates-only to isolate the recall signal from entity-routing behavior (entity-type tests live in their own bucket). Query is a slight paraphrase of John's actual title — uses 'OT/ICS' and 'industrial cybersecurity vendor' as semantic-only signals, since those exact phrases probably don't appear verbatim in his profile (Claroty does, but the query intentionally avoids naming it). If R1 fails, every downstream Recall test is suspect.

query: OT cybersecurity manager in industrial environments

positive_expectations.reason: Sr. Manager SCADA & Control System Design at SB Energy (SoftBank Group), category=engineering, seniority_level=manager, manages_people=true, San Diego CA, 11 work history rows, fresh embedding (April 29). His profile contains SCADA and 'control system' but does NOT contain 'OT' or 'operational technology' anywhere. Recall here requires the embedding model to map SCADA → OT and 'control system design' → 'OT cybersecurity' semantically. If he surfaces in top 5, the central thesis of Smart Search (semantic terminology mapping in OT/ICS) is working. If he doesn't, the value prop is broken — keyword-only would have caught the rest, but only semantic catches Charles.

negative_expectations.criteria: Pure IT cybersecurity managers (corporate IT security, SOC managers, CISO-track) with no industrial / OT / SCADA / control systems exposure in their profile
negative_expectations.reason: These will likely surface because 'cybersecurity manager' is a strong keyword + semantic signal. Whether they outrank Charles is the test — they should NOT, because the query specifies 'industrial environments' which is the asymmetric anchor pointing at the OT/SCADA cluster.

negative_expectations.criteria: Sales engineers and solution architects from OT vendors (Claroty, Dragos, Nozomi, etc.) who do NOT have manager-level seniority
negative_expectations.reason: OT-domain candidates with strong semantic match on 'OT' and 'industrial' but wrong on 'manager'. The query's 'manager' anchor should keep IC-level candidates from dominating, even if their OT terminology overlap is stronger than Charles's.

reasoning: R2 thesis test per audit plan §5A and §5E. Charles's profile is heavy on SCADA + control systems (legacy OT terminology) and contains zero instances of 'OT' or 'operational technology' (the modern terminology used in the query). Query intentionally avoids verbatim overlap with Charles's profile language — no 'SCADA', no 'control system', no 'energy/utilities'. For Smart Search to recall him, the embedding must perform the SCADA ↔ OT semantic mapping that justifies hybrid search over keyword-only. Metadata is aligned with query intent (category=engineering, seniority=manager, manages_people=true), so this is a clean isolation test of semantic asymmetry — not confounded by metadata mismatches. min_rank=5 is the strict bar — same as R1, despite R2 being intentionally harder by design. Triage caveat: if Charles fails to make top 5, the diagnosis is NOT automatically 'semantic is broken'. It could also be 'vocabulary distance is too aggressive for voyage-4-large to bridge in this specific case'. Disambiguate during triage by checking other SCADA-only candidates in the same query's top 10 — if any of them bridge and Charles doesn't, that's a Charles-specific data/embedding issue; if none of the SCADA-only candidates bridge, that's a systemic semantic gap. Charles's thin skills_chars (28) and certifications_chars (27) make him a title-and-history-driven profile, which is also worth noting for triage if he underperforms.

query: SCADA manager at an energy company

positive_expectations.reason: SCADA Manager at bp, category=engineering, seniority_level=manager, manages_people=true, Tulsa OK, 3 work history rows, headline_chars=19, skills_chars=36, certifications=0, fresh embedding (April 29). Sparse profile by design — the query keyword-aligns with his prominent signals (title + employer) but his profile has almost no supporting text beyond that. If he surfaces in top 5, sparse-but-prominent profiles are usable without enrichment. If he doesn't, sparsity is the failure mode and enrichment becomes mission-critical for bulk import. Failure diagnosis is clean: keyword overlap is high, so a miss cannot be blamed on vocabulary distance — only on thin embedding signal.

negative_expectations.criteria: Rich-profile SCADA candidates with deep work history, headline, and skills text who outrank Trent solely due to profile richness rather than relevance
negative_expectations.reason: If rich profiles systematically outrank sparse-but-accurate profiles, that's a data gap finding — the embedding is rewarding text volume over relevance. Trent's title and employer are as on-the-nose as it gets for this query; he should not be buried under richer profiles just because they have more text.

negative_expectations.criteria: Non-SCADA engineering managers in energy (e.g., mechanical, electrical, process engineering) with no OT/ICS/SCADA signal
negative_expectations.reason: Generic energy-company managers should not outrank a candidate whose title explicitly says SCADA Manager. If they do, keyword ranking is failing to anchor on 'SCADA' as a discriminating term.

reasoning: R3 sparse-data recall test per audit plan §5A and §5E. Trent's profile is genuinely sparse: 3 work history rows, 19-char headline, 36-char skills, zero certifications. Query is intentionally keyword-aligned with his prominent signals (SCADA + manager + energy) to isolate sparsity as the variable under test — vocabulary distance is NOT a factor here (contrast with R2 where vocabulary distance was the variable). Notes excluded (include_notes=false, scope=candidates) even though Trent's notes contain rich signal ('Experienced Cyber Security Engineer...oil & energy industry') — those notes would rescue him in notes-included mode, but R3 is testing the candidate-profile embedding in isolation. This test directly informs the workflow decision: if sparse profiles surface cleanly, the ~7k Apify bulk import can proceed without a pre-import enrichment pass; if they don't, enrichment is mission-critical before trusting search results on imported candidates. A failure here should be diagnosed by checking: (1) did any sparse profiles appear in the top 10, or only rich ones? (2) did Trent's embedding capture his title + employer as primary signals? Pattern evidence across the full audit run is stronger than single-candidate evidence.

query: OT cybersecurity engineer with manufacturing industry experience

positive_expectations.reason: Career spans steel mills (Primetals, AustralTek, Siemens), paper/cement (Siemens Germany), sugar manufacturing, petrochemical plants (ICA Fluor). Manufacturing woven across jobs 3-10. Current role is Sr Industrial Consultant at Dragos (OT cyber vendor). Tests whether embedding weights deep manufacturing history enough to surface her over the oil & gas / utility crowd.

negative_expectations.criteria: OT cybersecurity candidates whose entire career is oil & gas or electric utility with zero manufacturing history
negative_expectations.reason: Match the surface query (OT cybersecurity engineer) but fail the depth criterion (manufacturing). Should rank below Gloria.

negative_expectations.criteria: IT security professionals with no OT or manufacturing background
negative_expectations.reason: Fail both the surface criterion (OT cybersecurity) and the depth criterion (manufacturing).

reasoning: Tests career-arc depth weighting and vocabulary bridging. Gloria's profile never says 'manufacturing' explicitly — it says 'steel mills', 'processing lines', 'iron and steel industry', 'paper and cement', 'sugar manufacturing', 'petrochemical'. Embedding must connect those terms to the query word 'manufacturing'. Also a soft truncation probe — 10 work history entries with rich descriptions will push formatted text toward the rerank character budget. Deliberate opposite of R3 (sparse data).

query: Someone who secures industrial control rooms and plant networks

positive_expectations.reason: Current role at Claroty is exactly 'securing industrial control rooms and plant networks' despite zero jargon overlap with query. Same candidate as R1 — comparing R1 (direct vocabulary) vs R5 (paraphrased intent) results reveals whether embedding matches vocabulary or intent.

negative_expectations.criteria: Pure IT security engineers who have never worked in OT/ICS or industrial environments
negative_expectations.reason: Query says 'industrial control rooms and plant networks' — IT-only candidates should not match despite overlapping words like 'secures' and 'networks'.

negative_expectations.criteria: Industrial automation engineers with no security role or responsibility
negative_expectations.reason: Match 'industrial control rooms and plant networks' but fail the 'secures' criterion — they build/maintain, they don't protect.

reasoning: Tests semantic intent comprehension when zero standard OT/ICS/SCADA/cybersecurity vocabulary is used. Query is phrased the way a hiring manager might describe the role in casual conversation. Paired with R1 (same candidate, direct terminology) — if John surfaces on R1 but drops on R5, the embedding is matching vocabulary not intent. Second negative expectation tests precision: 'industrial control rooms' should not pull in non-security automation engineers.

query: Senior OT cybersecurity sales engineer in the Southeast who manages a team and has vendor-side experience

positive_expectations.reason: Matches all 5 cross-field signals: sales_engineering category, senior seniority_level, Southeast US location, manages_people = true, current company is an OT cyber vendor. No single field answers the query — embedding must fuse all five. Also previews filter interaction (location regex + candidate_hard filter should both fire).

negative_expectations.criteria: OT cybersecurity sales engineers outside the Southeast US
negative_expectations.reason: Match category, seniority, and vendor-side signals but fail location. Tests whether location signal carries weight in ranking.

negative_expectations.criteria: Junior or individual-contributor OT sales engineers in the Southeast who do not manage a team
negative_expectations.reason: Match location and category but fail seniority and manages_people. Tests whether the embedding distinguishes leadership from IC.

reasoning: Tests cross-field signal combination — the query touches seniority_level, category, location, manages_people, and work history (vendor-side) simultaneously. No single field is sufficient. Also doubles as a preview stress test for Filter/Routing bucket: location regex should fire for 'Southeast', candidate_hard filter should fire for 'manages a team' and possibly 'sales engineer'. Reveals whether filters and semantic ranking cooperate or conflict when a query is this specific.

query: Product marketing leader who managed OT security messaging and built enablement for sales engineers

positive_expectations.reason: Category is marketing, manages_people is true. If sales_engineering category filter falsely fires, Dan gets killed from results (he's marketing, not sales_engineering). His survival confirms the category filter did not over-fire on 'sales engineers' in the query. Query describes his actual role — Sr Director of Product Marketing at TXOne who built enablement FOR sales engineers.

negative_expectations.criteria: Sales engineers themselves dominating results
negative_expectations.reason: Query is about someone who built enablement FOR sales engineers, not a sales engineer. If results are mostly sales_engineering candidates, the category filter attracted the wrong category.

negative_expectations.criteria: Pure demand gen or content marketers with no OT/ICS domain experience
negative_expectations.reason: Query specifies OT security messaging. Generic marketers without OT context should rank below Dan.

reasoning: Tests filter false-positive on category. Three trigger words: 'leader' and 'managed' could fire manages_people (correct in this case — Dan does manage people), 'sales engineers' could fire sales_engineering category (INCORRECT — sales engineers are the audience, not the candidate's role). Primary pass/fail: _debug.parsed_filters should NOT show sales_engineering in categories[]. Secondary pass/fail: Dan (marketing category) survives in results.

query: OT cybersecurity product marketer in the Lone Star State

positive_expectations.reason: Located in Austin, Texas. 'Lone Star State' is a colloquial synonym for Texas that the location regex likely won't recognize. Tests whether semantic search compensates when the filter can't fire. Dan also matches the semantic intent (OT cybersecurity product marketer at TXOne).

negative_expectations.criteria: Candidates located outside Texas dominating the top results
negative_expectations.reason: If 'Lone Star State' is understood semantically, Texas candidates should rank higher even without the location filter firing.

negative_expectations.criteria: IT marketers with no OT/ICS domain experience
negative_expectations.reason: Query specifies OT cybersecurity — generic tech marketers should not surface.

reasoning: Tests location filter false-negative. parseLocationFilter() likely only matches literal state names and abbreviations — 'Lone Star State' should not fire it. Primary diagnostic: _debug.parsed_filters.location.fired (expected false). Secondary diagnostic: does Dan still surface via semantic ranking despite no filter assistance? If yes, semantic search compensates for regex gaps. If no, both systems miss synonyms — real gap. Also informs post-audit decision on whether to build NLP query parser vs keeping regex.

query: OT cybersecurity sales engineers

positive_expectations.reason: Category is sales_engineering. Query explicitly names the category. This is the happy-path control test — if the filter works correctly, Gary should surface easily.

negative_expectations.criteria: Candidates from non-sales-engineering categories (engineering, marketing, product, etc.) appearing in results
negative_expectations.reason: Query explicitly says 'sales engineers' — the category filter should fire and exclude non-SE candidates entirely, not just rank them lower.

negative_expectations.criteria: IT sales reps with no OT/ICS domain experience
negative_expectations.reason: Query specifies OT cybersecurity — generic tech sales should not surface.

reasoning: Happy-path control test for category filter. Query unambiguously names sales_engineering. Primary pass/fail: _debug.parsed_filters.candidate_hard.fired = true with sales_engineering in categories[]. Secondary: results dominated by sales_engineering candidates. This is the baseline — if explicit category intent doesn't fire the filter correctly, edge cases (F4-F6) are moot.

query: Technical people who demo OT security products to customers and help close deals

positive_expectations.reason: Gary is a sales engineer — exactly the role described by 'technical people who demo products and help close deals.' But the query never says 'sales engineer.' Tests whether semantic search surfaces him without filter assistance. Paired with F3 (same candidate, explicit category) — comparing results reveals filter vs semantic contribution.

negative_expectations.criteria: Pure software engineers or developers with no customer-facing or sales role
negative_expectations.reason: Query describes a customer-facing technical role (demos, closing deals). Backend engineers without sales motion should not surface.

negative_expectations.criteria: Account executives or pure sales reps with no technical depth
negative_expectations.reason: Query says 'technical people' — pure closers without demo capability should rank below SEs.

reasoning: Tests category-intent implicit. The query describes a sales engineer role without using the words 'sales engineer.' Category regex almost certainly will NOT fire (no keyword match). Primary diagnostic: _debug.parsed_filters.candidate_hard.fired — expected false. Secondary: does Gary still surface via semantic ranking despite no category filter? Paired with F3 (explicit) — if Gary surfaces on F3 but drops on F4, the system depends on filters for category routing and semantic search alone can't compensate. If he surfaces on both, semantic ranking handles implicit intent. Directly informs post-audit decision on NLP query parser.

query: Customer-facing technical leaders across OT cybersecurity

positive_expectations.reason: Sales engineering is customer-facing technical work. Gary should surface on a broad cross-category query. min_rank loosened to 10 because two candidates must both appear — wider net needed.

Channel Sales Director at Nozomi is customer-facing technical leadership. Different category than Gary (channel vs sales_engineering). Both should surface if the filter doesn't over-narrow to a single category.

negative_expectations.criteria: Results restricted to only one category (e.g., only sales_engineering or only channel)
negative_expectations.reason: Query says 'across OT cybersecurity' — intent is deliberately broad. If the category filter fires on a single category, it kills the cross-category intent and one of the two expected candidates disappears.

negative_expectations.criteria: Backend engineers or non-customer-facing roles
negative_expectations.reason: Query specifies customer-facing. Engineers who don't interact with customers should not dominate.

reasoning: Tests adjacent/cross-category behavior. Query intent spans multiple categories (sales_engineering, channel, possibly customer_success). If the category filter fires on just one, it over-narrows and kills valid results from other categories. Primary diagnostic: _debug.parsed_filters.candidate_hard.fired — ideally false (no single category should dominate). Secondary: both Gary (sales_engineering) and Jake (channel) survive in top 10. min_rank loosened to 10 for both since the pool is broader. Informs post-audit decision on whether category filter needs a multi-category mode.

query: OT security engineers in Georgia

negative_expectations.criteria: OT security engineers located outside Georgia appearing in results
negative_expectations.reason: Location filter should fire and exclude non-Georgia candidates entirely, not just rank them lower.

negative_expectations.criteria: Non-OT/non-security candidates in Georgia
negative_expectations.reason: Location filter narrows geography but semantic ranking should still enforce OT security relevance.

Secondary: results restricted to Georgia candidates. Paired with F2 (location synonym 'Lone Star State') — F6 is the baseline. If explicit state names don't fire the filter correctly, synonym handling is moot.

negative_expectations.reason: Query explicitly says VP — mid-level management should not outrank an actual VP

The formatter includes seniority_level and manages_people as labeled fields, but they are a small fraction of the total embedded text. If ICs with rich OT skills sections outrank Chet, the seniority signal is being drowned out by keyword density.

query: experienced OT cybersecurity sales leader with engineering background

positive_expectations.reason: Head of Commercial Sales at Galvanick, previously VP at Secomea and Xage, Sr. Director at Nozomi, started career as an engineer — profile is saturated with OT cybersecurity sales evidence across multiple vendors plus engineering roots. If anyone scores STRONG on this query, it's Brett.

negative_expectations.criteria: Pure IT sales reps with no OT/ICS vendor history or engineering background
negative_expectations.reason: Query specifies OT cybersecurity and engineering background — generic IT sales should not score STRONG

negative_expectations.criteria: Engineers with no sales leadership history who happen to work at OT vendors
negative_expectations.reason: Query specifies sales leader — pure ICs should not score higher than someone who has led sales at multiple OT companies

reasoning: Primary calibration test. Brett's profile is a near-perfect match for this query across every dimension — OT domain, sales leadership, engineering background, multi-vendor career. Tests whether STRONG label (rerank ≥ 0.90) fires on a known slam-dunk match. If Brett does not receive STRONG, the 0.90 threshold is too tight and needs recalibrating.

query: OT sales engineer for industrial cybersecurity vendor

positive_expectations.reason: Systems Engineer II at RoviSys with deep OT/ICS/SCADA/PLC skills — strong domain match but has never done sales or security work. Should appear as a GOOD match (0.85-0.89 rerank score) but NOT STRONG (< 0.90). If STRONG fires, thresholds are too generous. If Possible Match fires, thresholds are too stingy.

negative_expectations.criteria: Pure IT sales engineers with no OT/ICS/SCADA experience
negative_expectations.reason: They match the sales engineer function but lack the OT domain — should not outrank someone with genuine OT controls experience

negative_expectations.criteria: OT professionals with neither sales nor engineering in their background (e.g., OT project managers or compliance analysts)
negative_expectations.reason: Query specifically asks for sales engineer — adjacent OT roles without SE function should rank below Jackson

reasoning: Tests GOOD threshold calibration. Jackson is a deliberate partial match — right domain (OT/ICS/SCADA), wrong function (no sales, no security), with misleading keyword bait ('SALES ENGINEERING' in skills despite no SE experience). If rerank scores him STRONG, the model is over-weighting keyword overlap. If he gets Possible Match, the model is penalizing missing function too harshly. Ideal outcome: GOOD label, ranking below actual OT SEs but above pure-IT candidates.

query: OT cybersecurity incident response engineer

positive_expectations.reason: Global Compliance Manager at Nozomi Networks — works at an OT cybersecurity vendor and has OT cybersecurity/ICS security keywords in skills, but actual work is compliance/ISO audits/IT infrastructure. Should appear as Possible Match at best (rerank < 0.85). If GOOD or STRONG fires, thresholds are too generous and the reranker is being fooled by surface-level keyword overlap.

negative_expectations.criteria: Frank Dellé receiving a GOOD (≥ 0.85) or STRONG (≥ 0.90) match label
negative_expectations.reason: His actual work is compliance and IT management — OT cybersecurity keywords in skills are borrowed from his employer context, not his function. A GOOD or STRONG label here means the system cannot distinguish doing OT cybersecurity from working near it.

negative_expectations.criteria: Other compliance, HR, or administrative professionals at OT vendors outranking actual OT cybersecurity engineers
negative_expectations.reason: Employer-inherited keywords should not elevate non-technical roles above genuine OT incident response candidates

reasoning: Tests the floor of the calibration range. Frank is a deliberately weak match — right company (Nozomi), right keywords in skills (OT cybersecurity, ICS security), but completely wrong function (compliance/IT, not incident response engineering). The OT keywords were added to his skills because he works at Nozomi, making this a hard test of whether rerank sees through keyword bait to actual work history. If he scores GOOD or above, the thresholds need tightening.
